summary: Graham argues that Microsoft’s dominance effectively ended by the mid‑2000s, not because it stopped making money, but because it stopped shaping the software world. He credits Google’s rise, the shift to web apps via Ajax, broadband, and Apple’s OS X comeback as the converging forces that made the desktop era—and Microsoft’s centrality—obsolete.
key_points: Microsoft’s power faded when Google became the new “big man” and set the agenda for software innovation.
Web-based applications (Ajax) and broadband made the desktop less central, undercutting Microsoft’s core advantage.
Apple’s OS X resurgence shifted influential users away from Windows, isolating Microsoft culturally and technologically.
Microsoft could theoretically buy its way back into relevance, but its internal culture prevents that reset.
money_quotes: But it's gone now. I can sense that. No one is even afraid of Microsoft anymore.
There can only be one big man in town, and they're clearly it.
Gmail showed they could do more than search.
Everyone can see the desktop is over.
Microsoft's biggest weakness is that they still don't realize how much they suck.
key_insight: Microsoft’s decline wasn’t about profits but about losing technological and cultural dominance as the center of software shifted to the web and to new leaders.
